  30 |     """Raised when a candidate tries to score more than one equation template."""
  31 | 
  32 | 
  33 | MAX_EQUATION_COMPLEXITY = int(os.environ.get("SKYDISCOVER_MAX_EQUATION_COMPLEXITY", "160"))
  34 | MAX_EQUATION_CONSTANTS = int(os.environ.get("SKYDISCOVER_MAX_EQUATION_CONSTANTS", "12"))
  35 | _BANNED_EXPR_TYPES = (
  36 |     sp.Piecewise,
  37 |     Relational,
  38 |     BooleanFunction,
  39 |     sp.Heaviside,
  40 |     sp.DiracDelta,
  41 | )
  42 | _BANNED_EXPR_FUNCTIONS = {
  43 |     "Max",
  44 |     "Min",
  45 |     "sign",
  46 |     "floor",
  47 |     "ceiling",
  48 |     "Mod",
  49 | }
  50 | 
  51 | 
  52 | @dataclass(frozen=True)
  53 | class FittedExpression:
  54 |     """A fitted symbolic expression and its validation predictions."""
  55 | 
  56 |     expression_template: sp.Expr
  57 |     expression: sp.Expr
  58 |     constants: dict[str, float]
  59 |     y_pred_train: NDArray
  60 |     y_pred_val: NDArray
  61 | 
  62 | 
  63 | @dataclass
  64 | class _SingleEquationState:
  65 |     calls: int = 0
  66 |     template_fingerprint: str | None = None
  67 |     result_snapshot: dict[str, Any] | None = None
  68 | 
  69 | 
  70 | _SINGLE_EQUATION_STATE = threading.local()
  71 | 
  72 | 
  73 | @contextmanager
  74 | def single_equation_evaluation():
  75 |     """
  76 |     Enforce the benchmark contract for one evaluate_symbolic_candidate() call.
  77 | 
  78 |     A candidate may build helper subexpressions, but it may submit exactly one
  79 |     symbolic template to the scorer. This prevents reward hacking where a
  80 |     program tries many equations and returns whichever receives the best score.
  81 |     """
  82 |     previous = getattr(_SINGLE_EQUATION_STATE, "state", None)
  83 |     state = _SingleEquationState()
  84 |     _SINGLE_EQUATION_STATE.state = state
  85 |     try:
  86 |         yield
  87 |         if state.calls != 1:
  88 |             raise SingleEquationViolation(
  89 |                 "Single-equation violation: evaluate_symbolic_candidate() must call "
  90 |                 "evaluate_expression() exactly once with one symbolic equation template. "
  91 |                 f"It submitted {state.calls} templates. Build one base equation and let "
  92 |                 "the harness fit that equation's constants per dataset."
  93 |             )
  94 |     finally:
  95 |         _SINGLE_EQUATION_STATE.state = previous
  96 | 
  97 | 
  98 | def _record_scored_template(template: sp.Expr) -> None:
  99 |     state = getattr(_SINGLE_EQUATION_STATE, "state", None)
 100 |     if state is None:
 101 |         return
 102 | 
 103 |     state.calls += 1
 104 |     fingerprint = sp.srepr(template)
 105 |     if state.template_fingerprint is None:
 106 |         state.template_fingerprint = fingerprint
 107 |         return
 108 | 
 109 |     if state.template_fingerprint != fingerprint:
 110 |         raise SingleEquationViolation(
 111 |             "Single-equation violation: one evaluate_symbolic_candidate() call "
 112 |             "submitted multiple different symbolic equation templates. The base "
 113 |             "equation must be fixed; only fitted constants may change."
 114 |         )
 115 | 
 116 |     raise SingleEquationViolation(
 117 |         "Single-equation violation: evaluate_symbolic_candidate() called "
 118 |         "evaluate_expression()/fit_expression_constants() more than once. Do not "
 119 |         "try multiple equation templates or multiple scorer calls and pick the "
 120 |         "best result; submit exactly one symbolic expression."
 121 |     )
 122 | 
 123 | 
 124 | def _record_scorer_result(result: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
 125 |     state = getattr(_SINGLE_EQUATION_STATE, "state", None)
 126 |     if state is not None:
 127 |         state.result_snapshot = copy.deepcopy(result)
 128 | 
 129 | 
 130 | def validate_single_equation_result(result: Any) -> None:
 131 |     """
 132 |     Ensure the candidate returns the actual scorer output, unchanged.
 133 | 
 134 |     This closes the loophole where a program calls evaluate_expression() once
 135 |     to satisfy the call-count guard, then fabricates or edits the metrics it
 136 |     returns to the evaluator.
 137 |     """
 138 |     state = getattr(_SINGLE_EQUATION_STATE, "state", None)
 139 |     if state is None:
 140 |         return
 141 | 
 142 |     if state.result_snapshot is None:
 143 |         raise SingleEquationViolation(
 144 |             "Single-equation violation: evaluate_symbolic_candidate() must return "
 145 |             "the dictionary produced by evaluate_expression()."
 146 |         )
 147 | 
 148 |     if result != state.result_snapshot:
 149 |         raise SingleEquationViolation(
 150 |             "Single-equation violation: evaluate_symbolic_candidate() must return "
 151 |             "the exact, unmodified result from its single evaluate_expression() "
 152 |             "call. Do not fabricate, overwrite, wrap, or post-process metrics."
 153 |         )
 154 | 
 155 | 
 156 | def feature_symbols(n_features: int) -> list[sp.Symbol]:
 157 |     """Create PySR-compatible feature symbols x0, x1, ..."""
 158 |     return create_sympy_symbols([f"x{i}" for i in range(n_features)])
 159 | 
 160 | 
 161 | def constant_symbols(n_constants: int) -> list[sp.Symbol]:
 162 |     """Create tunable constant symbols c0, c1, ..."""
 163 |     return [sp.Symbol(f"c{i}", real=True) for i in range(n_constants)]
 164 | 
 165 | 
 166 | def pysr_operator_names() -> dict[str, list[str]]:
 167 |     """Return the PySR operator vocabulary exposed to evolved equation builders."""
 168 |     return {
 169 |         "unary": GENERAL_UNARY_OPERATORS.copy(),
 170 |         "binary": GENERAL_BINARY_OPERATORS.copy(),
 171 |         "ternary": GENERAL_TERNARY_OPERATORS.copy(),
 172 |     }
 173 | 
 174 | 
 175 | def pysr_operator_namespace() -> dict[str, Any]:
 176 |     """
 177 |     Return PySR-to-SymPy operator callables usable in expression templates.
 178 | 
 179 |     Operators like ``+``, ``-``, ``*``, and ``/`` should be written with normal
 180 |     Python syntax. Named PySR operators such as ``sin``, ``cos``, ``sqrt``,
 181 |     ``log1p``, ``max``, ``min``, ``muladd``, and ``clamp`` are exposed here.
 182 |     """
 183 |     return {
 184 |         **sympy_mappings,
 185 |         "pi": sp.pi,
 186 |         "E": sp.E,
 187 |     }
 188 | 
 189 | 
 190 | def _as_sympy_expr(expression: sp.Expr | str | float | int, feature_names: Sequence[str]) -> sp.Expr:
 191 |     if isinstance(expression, sp.Expr):
 192 |         return expression
 193 |     return pysr2sympy(expression, feature_names_in=list(feature_names))
 194 | 
 195 | 
 196 | def _complexity(expression: sp.Expr) -> float:
 197 |     return float(sum(1 for _ in sp.preorder_traversal(expression)))
 198 | 
 199 | 
 200 | def _validate_expression_template(template: sp.Expr, constants: Sequence[sp.Symbol]) -> None:
 201 |     complexity = int(_complexity(template))
 202 |     if complexity > MAX_EQUATION_COMPLEXITY:
 203 |         raise SingleEquationViolation(
 204 |             "Single-equation violation: symbolic template is too complex "
 205 |             f"({complexity} nodes > limit {MAX_EQUATION_COMPLEXITY}). Do not hide "
 206 |             "many candidate equations inside one oversized expression."
 207 |         )
 208 | 
 209 |     if len(constants) > MAX_EQUATION_CONSTANTS:
 210 |         raise SingleEquationViolation(
 211 |             "Single-equation violation: too many fitted constants "
 212 |             f"({len(constants)} > limit {MAX_EQUATION_CONSTANTS}). Use one compact "
 213 |             "universal equation, not an over-parameterized surrogate."
 214 |         )
 215 | 
 216 |     for node in sp.preorder_traversal(template):
 217 |         if isinstance(node, _BANNED_EXPR_TYPES) or node.func.__name__ in _BANNED_EXPR_FUNCTIONS:
 218 |             raise SingleEquationViolation(
 219 |                 "Single-equation violation: conditional, piecewise, discontinuous, "
 220 |                 f"or gated symbolic construct '{node.func.__name__}' is not allowed. "
 221 |                 "Use one smooth global equation template."
 222 |             )
 223 | 
 224 | 
 225 | def _predict(expression: sp.Expr, X: NDArray, feature_names: Sequence[str]) -> NDArray:
 226 |     symbols = create_sympy_symbols(feature_names)
 227 |     fn = sympy2numpy(expression, symbols)
 228 |     with np.errstate(over="ignore", divide="ignore", invalid="ignore"):
 229 |         if isinstance(fn, CallableEquation):
 230 |             y_pred = fn(np.asarray(X, dtype=float))
 231 |         else:
 232 |             y_pred = fn(*[X[:, i] for i in range(X.shape[1])])
 233 |     return np.asarray(y_pred, dtype=float).reshape(-1)
 234 | 
 235 | 
 236 | def fit_expression_constants(
 237 |     expression: sp.Expr | str | float | int,
 238 |     X_train: NDArray,
 239 |     y_train: NDArray,
 240 |     X_val: NDArray,
 241 |     *,
 242 |     constants: Sequence[sp.Symbol] | None = None,
 243 |     initial_values: Sequence[float] | None = None,
 244 |     max_nfev: int = 300,
 245 | ) -> FittedExpression:
 246 |     """
 247 |     Fit free constants in a SkyDiscover-proposed expression template.
 248 | 
 249 |     The expression structure is supplied by the evolved program. This helper only
 250 |     performs the continuous inner fit and PySR-style export/evaluation.
 251 |     """
 252 |     X_train = np.asarray(X_train, dtype=float)
 253 |     y_train = np.asarray(y_train, dtype=float).reshape(-1)
 254 |     X_val = np.asarray(X_val, dtype=float)
 255 |     feature_names = [f"x{i}" for i in range(X_train.shape[1])]
 256 |     template = _as_sympy_expr(expression, feature_names)
 257 |     _record_scored_template(template)
 258 | 
 259 |     if constants is None:
 260 |         feature_set = set(feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1]))
 261 |         constants = sorted(template.free_symbols - feature_set, key=lambda s: s.name)
 262 |     constants = list(constants)
 263 |     _validate_expression_template(template, constants)
 264 | 
 265 |     if not constants:
 266 |         return FittedExpression(
 267 |             expression_template=template,
 268 |             expression=template,
 269 |             constants={},
 270 |             y_pred_train=_predict(template, X_train, feature_names),
 271 |             y_pred_val=_predict(template, X_val, feature_names),
 272 |         )
 273 | 
 274 |     if initial_values is None:
 275 |         x0 = np.ones(len(constants), dtype=float)
 276 |     else:
 277 |         x0 = np.asarray(initial_values, dtype=float).reshape(-1)
 278 |         if x0.size != len(constants):
 279 |             raise ValueError("initial_values must match the number of constants")
 280 | 
 281 |     feature_syms = create_sympy_symbols(feature_names)
 282 |     X_cols = [X_train[:, i] for i in range(X_train.shape[1])]
 283 | 
 284 |     # Compile expression once upfront so the residual avoids repeated
 285 |     # SymPy subs() + sympy2numpy() calls (the main bottleneck).
 286 |     try:
 287 |         _compiled = sp.lambdify(feature_syms + constants, template, modules=["numpy"])
 288 |         _test_args = X_cols + [float(v) for v in x0]
 289 |         _test_out = np.asarray(_compiled(*_test_args), dtype=float).reshape(-1)
 290 |         if _test_out.shape != y_train.shape:
 291 |             raise ValueError("shape mismatch")
 292 |         _use_compiled = True
 293 |     except Exception:
 294 |         _use_compiled = False
 295 | 
 296 |     if _use_compiled:
 297 |         def residual(theta: NDArray) -> NDArray:
 298 |             try:
 299 |                 args = X_cols + [float(v) for v in theta]
 300 |                 prediction = np.asarray(_compiled(*args), dtype=float).reshape(-1)
 301 |             except Exception:
 302 |                 return np.full_like(y_train, 1e12, dtype=float)
 303 |             if prediction.shape != y_train.shape or not np.all(np.isfinite(prediction)):
 304 |                 return np.full_like(y_train, 1e12, dtype=float)
 305 |             return prediction - y_train
 306 |     else:
 307 |         def residual(theta: NDArray) -> NDArray:
 308 |             substitutions = {symbol: float(value) for symbol, value in zip(constants, theta)}
 309 |             candidate = template.subs(substitutions)
 310 |             try:
 311 |                 prediction = _predict(candidate, X_train, feature_names)
 312 |             except Exception:
 313 |                 return np.full_like(y_train, 1e12, dtype=float)
 314 |             if prediction.shape != y_train.shape or not np.all(np.isfinite(prediction)):
 315 |                 return np.full_like(y_train, 1e12, dtype=float)
 316 |             return prediction - y_train
 317 | 
 318 |     import warnings
 319 | 
 320 |     with warnings.catch_warnings(), np.errstate(over="ignore", divide="ignore", invalid="ignore"):
 321 |         warnings.simplefilter("ignore", RuntimeWarning)
 322 |         result = least_squares(
 323 |             residual,
 324 |             x0,
 325 |             loss="soft_l1",
 326 |             max_nfev=max_nfev,
 327 |         )
 328 |     fitted_constants = {
 329 |         str(symbol): float(value) for symbol, value in zip(constants, result.x)
 330 |     }
 331 |     substitutions = dict(zip(constants, result.x))
 332 |     fitted_expr = template.subs(substitutions)
 333 | 
 334 |     return FittedExpression(
 335 |         expression_template=template,
 336 |         expression=fitted_expr,
 337 |         constants=fitted_constants,
 338 |         y_pred_train=_predict(fitted_expr, X_train, feature_names),
 339 |         y_pred_val=_predict(fitted_expr, X_val, feature_names),
 340 |     )
 341 | 
 342 | 
 343 | def evaluate_expression(
 344 |     expression: sp.Expr | str | float | int,
 345 |     X_train: NDArray,
 346 |     y_train: NDArray,
 347 |     X_val: NDArray,
 348 |     y_val: NDArray,
 349 |     *,
 350 |     constants: Sequence[sp.Symbol] | None = None,
 351 |     initial_values: Sequence[float] | None = None,
 352 |     max_nfev: int = 300,
 353 | ) -> dict[str, Any]:
 354 |     """Score a proposed equation template after fitting its constants."""
 355 |     y_train = np.asarray(y_train, dtype=float).reshape(-1)
 356 |     y_val = np.asarray(y_val, dtype=float).reshape(-1)
 357 |     fitted = fit_expression_constants(
 358 |         expression,
 359 |         X_train,
 360 |         y_train,
 361 |         X_val,
 362 |         constants=constants,
 363 |         initial_values=initial_values,
 364 |         max_nfev=max_nfev,
 365 |     )
 366 |     nmse_train = nmse(y_train, fitted.y_pred_train)
 367 |     nmse_val = nmse(y_val, fitted.y_pred_val)
 368 |     loss = float(np.mean((fitted.y_pred_train - y_train) ** 2))
 369 | 
 370 |     result = {
 371 |         "equation_template": str(fitted.expression_template),
 372 |         "equation": str(fitted.expression),
 373 |         "constants": fitted.constants,
 374 |         "loss": loss,
 375 |         "complexity": _complexity(fitted.expression_template),
 376 |         "nmse_train": nmse_train,
 377 |         "nmse_val": nmse_val,
 378 |         "combined_score": combined_score_from_nmse(nmse_val),
 379 |     }
 380 |     _record_scorer_result(result)
 381 |     return result

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, '.')

from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

datasets = load_all_datasets()
print(f'Loaded {len(datasets)} datasets')

# Current expression
def eval_current(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val):
    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
    c = constant_symbols(9)
    time = x[0]
    parameter = x[1]
    log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))
    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
    rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time)
    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
        constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)

results = []
for name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:
    r = eval_current(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
    results.append((r['nmse_val'], name))

results.sort(reverse=True)
print('Worst 15 datasets (nmse_val):')
for nmse, name in results[:15]:
    print(f'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}')
print()
print(f'Mean nmse_val: {np.mean([r[0] for r in results]):.4f}')

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import os

base = '/home/mrsar/protein-ag/past-published-data/disease-relevant non-inhibited'

# Examine the worst datasets
hard_datasets = [
    'insulin/Fits/Nielsen2001_seeded_sec',
    'htt/Kar2011/Fits/Kar2011_Q37_sec',
    'serum amyloid/Ye2011/Fits/Ye2011_sec',
    'htt/Kar2011/Fits/Kar2011_Q37_prim',
]

for ds in hard_datasets:
    path = os.path.join(base, ds, 'fit.tsv')
    df = pd.read_csv(path, sep='\t')
    print(f'=== {ds} ===')
    print(f'  shape: {df.shape}, cols: {list(df.columns)}')
    x0 = df.iloc[:,0].values
    print(f'  x0: [{x0.min():.4g}, {x0.max():.4g}]')
    # Print first few rows
    print(df.head(5).to_string())

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import os, sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from pathlib import Path
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import _load_fit_table, load_dataset

base = '/home/mrsar/protein-ag/past-published-data/disease-relevant non-inhibited'

# Insulin seeded - look at actual y values vs x0
path = Path(base) / 'insulin/Fits/Nielsen2001_seeded_sec/fit.tsv'
X, y = _load_fit_table(path)
# Sort by x0 for each x1
for x1_val in np.unique(X[:,1]):
    mask = X[:,1] == x1_val
    x0s = X[mask,0]
    ys = y[mask]
    idx = np.argsort(x0s)
    print(f'x1={x1_val}: first 8 (x0,y):', list(zip(x0s[idx[:8]].round(0), ys[idx[:8]].round(4))))
    print(f'  last 4:', list(zip(x0s[idx[-4:]].round(0), ys[idx[-4:]].round(4))))
print()

# Kar2011 Q37 - look at a few curves
path2 = Path(base) / 'htt/Kar2011/Fits/Kar2011_Q37_sec/fit.tsv'
X2, y2 = _load_fit_table(path2)
for x1_val in sorted(np.unique(X2[:,1]))[:3]:
    mask = X2[:,1] == x1_val
    x0s = X2[mask,0]
    ys = y2[mask]
    idx = np.argsort(x0s)
    print(f'Kar Q37 x1={x1_val}: first 5:', list(zip(x0s[idx[:5]].round(0), ys[idx[:5]].round(4))))
    print(f'  last 3:', list(zip(x0s[idx[-3:]].round(0), ys[idx[-3:]].round(4))))
"

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from pathlib import Path
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import _load_fit_table, load_dataset
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

base = '/home/mrsar/protein-ag/past-published-data/disease-relevant non-inhibited'

# Test the insulin seeded dataset with current expression
path = Path(base) / 'insulin/Fits/Nielsen2001_seeded_sec/fit.tsv'
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = load_dataset(path)
print('Insulin seeded:')
print('  X_train x1 unique:', np.unique(X_train[:,1]))
print('  X_train shape:', X_train.shape)
print('  y_train range:', y_train.min(), y_train.max())

# The issue: all 3 curves have x1=344. The 3 curves have DIFFERENT shapes
# but x1 can't distinguish them.
# The 'no seeds' curve rises very slowly (nearly zero for a long time)
# The seeded curves rise faster.
# This is fundamentally multi-curve with same x1 - our model can't distinguish them!

# Let's see what the fitted constants look like
x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
c = constant_symbols(9)
time = x[0]
parameter = x[1]
log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))
log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time)
r = evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
    constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)
print('  nmse_val:', r['nmse_val'])
print('  constants:', r['constants'])
"
[mode: exec]
[exit code: 0]

  X_train x1 unique: [344.]
  X_train shape: (225, 2)
  y_train range: 0.0 1.0

  constants: {'c0': 1.2308062417444297, 'c1': 0.7977737121714922, 'c2': 1.7381445320877373, 'c3': 1.5501137993568088, 'c4': 1.4595713951106422, 'c5': 2.3313714230956037, 'c6': 1.2227762048738446, 'c7': -0.3297668922647038, 'c8': -0.19459958638414226}

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from pathlib import Path
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_dataset
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

base = '/home/mrsar/protein-ag/past-published-data/disease-relevant non-inhibited'

# Kar2011 Q37 - understand the shape
path = Path(base) / 'htt/Kar2011/Fits/Kar2011_Q37_sec/fit.tsv'
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = load_dataset(path)
print('Kar2011 Q37 sec:')
print('  x1 unique:', np.unique(X_train[:,1]))
print('  x0 range:', X_train[:,0].min(), X_train[:,0].max())

# What does the curve look like for x1=22 (highest, fastest)?
mask = X_train[:,1] == 22
x0s = X_train[mask,0]
ys = y_train[mask]
idx = np.argsort(x0s)
print('  x1=22 curve (first 5):', list(zip(x0s[idx[:5]].round(0), ys[idx[:5]].round(4))))
print('  x1=22 curve (last 3):', list(zip(x0s[idx[-3:]].round(0), ys[idx[-3:]].round(4))))

# For x1=3 (slowest)?
mask2 = X_train[:,1] == 3
x0s2 = X_train[mask2,0]
ys2 = y_train[mask2]
idx2 = np.argsort(x0s2)
print('  x1=3 curve (first 5):', list(zip(x0s2[idx2[:5]].round(0), ys2[idx2[:5]].round(4))))
print('  x1=3 curve (last 3):', list(zip(x0s2[idx2[-3:]].round(0), ys2[idx2[-3:]].round(4))))

# Test current expression
x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
c = constant_symbols(9)
time = x[0]
parameter = x[1]
log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))
log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time)
r = evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
    constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)
print('  nmse_val:', r['nmse_val'])
print('  constants:', r['constants'])

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from pathlib import Path
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_dataset
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

base = '/home/mrsar/protein-ag/past-published-data/disease-relevant non-inhibited'

# Test with a modified expression on the 3 hardest datasets
# Key insight: Kar2011 has sequential x1 (3-22) and slow power-law growth
# The issue: tanh(x0) secondary term uses raw x0, which for x0 in [7700, 937000] 
# is always saturated at 1. So c7*tanh(x0) = c7 (just a constant offset)
# This means we have 9 constants but effectively 8 for this dataset.

# Let's try with c7*tanh(c9*x0) - adaptive timescale for secondary ramp
# But that would need 10 constants

# Alternative: use x0^2 term to capture the power-law-like early growth
# Kar2011 curves look like they grow as x0^n (polynomial) not sigmoidal at early times

# Check: does the Kar2011 Q37 data actually look sigmoidal?
path = Path(base) / 'htt/Kar2011/Fits/Kar2011_Q37_sec/fit.tsv'
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = load_dataset(path)

# For x1=22 (fastest curve), look at y vs x0 shape
mask = X_train[:,1] == 22
x0s = X_train[mask,0]
ys = y_train[mask]
idx = np.argsort(x0s)
x0s_s = x0s[idx]
ys_s = ys[idx]
# Check log-log relationship in early phase
early = ys_s < 0.5
print('x1=22 early growth (log x0 vs log y):')
for x0, y in zip(x0s_s[early][:8], ys_s[early][:8]):
    if y > 0:
        print(f'  log10(x0)={np.log10(x0):.2f}, log10(y)={np.log10(y):.2f}')

08:38:54 [run_command_tool] run_command: executing 'python3 -c "\nimport numpy as np\nimport sympy as sp\nimport sys\nsys.path.insert(0, \'.\')\nfrom pathlib import Path\nfrom disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_dataset, load_all_datasets\nfrom pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols\n\ndatasets = load_all_datasets()\n\n# Test candidate: replace c7*tanh(x0) with c7*tanh(x0/c5) \n# But c5 is already used for half-log-time offset.\n# Instead: use c7*tanh(x0*c8) where c8 replaces the power-law exponent\n# We need 10 constants for that. Let\'s check if 10 constants is within limit (12 max).\n\n# Strategy: drop the c8 power-law on x1 (set it to 0 effectively) \n# and use that slot for the timescale of the secondary ramp.\n# New template (9 constants):\n# c0*tanh(c1*(log(x0+sqrt(x0^2+c2)) - c3*log(x1+c4) - c5)) + c6 + c7*tanh(c8*x0)\n# where c8 now controls the timescale of the secondary ramp\n# This loses the x1 power-law on rate, but gains adaptive ramp timescale\n\ndef eval_v2(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val):\n    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])\n    c = constant_symbols(9)\n    time = x[0]\n    parameter = x[1]\n    log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))\n    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])\n    rate = c[1]  # No x1 power law - simpler\n    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]\n    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)\n    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(c[8] * time)\n    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,\n        constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.001], max_nfev=1000)\n\nresults = []\nfor name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:\n    r = eval_v2(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)\n    results.append((r[\'nmse_val\'], name))\n\nresults.sort(reverse=True)\nprint(\'Worst 10 (v2 - no x1 power law, adaptive ramp):\')\nfor nmse, name in results[:10]:\n    print(f\'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}\')\nprint(f\'Mean nmse_val: {np.mean([r[0] for r in results]):.4f}\')\n" 2>&1 | tail -15' in /home/mrsar/protein-ag/skydiscover/benchmarks/pysr_symbolic (timeout=120s)
08:39:10 [agentic_generator] Step 14: tool=run_command returned:
$ python3 -c "
import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from pathlib import Path
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_dataset, load_all_datasets
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

datasets = load_all_datasets()

# Test candidate: replace c7*tanh(x0) with c7*tanh(x0/c5) 
# But c5 is already used for half-log-time offset.
# Instead: use c7*tanh(x0*c8) where c8 replaces the power-law exponent
# We need 10 constants for that. Let's check if 10 constants is within limit (12 max).

# Strategy: drop the c8 power-law on x1 (set it to 0 effectively) 
# and use that slot for the timescale of the secondary ramp.
# New template (9 constants):
# c0*tanh(c1*(log(x0+sqrt(x0^2+c2)) - c3*log(x1+c4) - c5)) + c6 + c7*tanh(c8*x0)
# where c8 now controls the timescale of the secondary ramp
# This loses the x1 power-law on rate, but gains adaptive ramp timescale

def eval_v2(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val):
    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
    c = constant_symbols(9)
    time = x[0]
    parameter = x[1]
    log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))
    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
    rate = c[1]  # No x1 power law - simpler
    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(c[8] * time)
    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
        constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.001], max_nfev=1000)

results = []
for name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:
    r = eval_v2(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
    results.append((r['nmse_val'], name))

results.sort(reverse=True)
print('Worst 10 (v2 - no x1 power law, adaptive ramp):')
for nmse, name in results[:10]:
    print(f'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}')
print(f'Mean nmse_val: {np.mean([r[0] for r in results]):.4f}')
" 2>&1 | tail -15

08:39:40 [agentic_generator] Step 15: tool=run_command returned:
$ python3 -c "
import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

datasets = load_all_datasets()

# 10-constant version: keeps x1 power-law AND adaptive ramp timescale
# c0*tanh(c1*x1^c9*(log(x0+sqrt(x0^2+c2)) - c3*log(x1+c4) - c5)) + c6 + c7*tanh(c8*x0)
def eval_v3(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val):
    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
    c = constant_symbols(10)
    time = x[0]
    parameter = x[1]
    log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))
    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
    rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[9]
    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(c[8] * time)
    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
        constants=c,
        initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.001, 0.0],
        max_nfev=1000)

results = []
for name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:
    r = eval_v3(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
    results.append((r['nmse_val'], name))

results.sort(reverse=True)
print('Worst 10 (v3 - x1 power law + adaptive ramp, 10 constants):')
for nmse, name in results[:10]:
    print(f'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}')
print(f'Mean nmse_val: {np.mean([r[0] for r in results]):.4f}')
print(f'Complexity: {results[0][0]}')  # just to check
# get complexity from first result
" 2>&1 | tail -15

08:40:17 [agentic_generator] Step 16: tool=run_command returned:
$ python3 -c "
import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

datasets = load_all_datasets()

# Get complexity for v3
name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = datasets[0]
x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
c = constant_symbols(10)
time = x[0]
parameter = x[1]
log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))
log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[9]
half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(c[8] * time)
r = evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
    constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.001, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)
print('v3 complexity:', r['complexity'])

# Also test: what if we use a simpler adaptive ramp - just scale the log_time differently?
# The key problem: tanh(x0) for large x0 is always 1, making c7 just an offset
# that duplicates c6. This wastes a degree of freedom.
# What about: remove c7*tanh(x0) entirely, and instead use a 2-term sum of sigmoids?
# c0*tanh(c1*x1^c8*(log(x0+sqrt(x0^2+c2)) - c3*log(x1+c4) - c5)) + c6
# That's only 7 constants, which might underfit.

# Let's try: use the log-time transform for the secondary ramp too
# c0*tanh(c1*x1^c8*(log(x0+sqrt(x0^2+c2)) - c3*log(x1+c4) - c5)) + c6 + c7*tanh(c8*log(x0+sqrt(x0^2+c2)))
# But that reuses c2 and adds complexity

# Better idea: the secondary ramp is for datasets where y starts at nonzero
# We could just use a baseline offset c6 + c7*x0 (linear ramp)
# But that won't saturate at 1

# Most principled: the issue is that for seeded datasets, the curve rises immediately
# rather than having a lag phase. The tanh(x0) captures this only when x0~1.
# For large x0, it just becomes a constant.
# But actually: for seeded datasets, c7 ≈ 0 and the main sigmoid handles it.
# The tanh(x0) term is mainly useful for prp/Stoehr (x0 in [1e-14, 17]) and alphasyn.

# Let's check: what datasets benefit most from c7*tanh(x0)?
print()
print('Testing without secondary ramp (7 constants):')
results_no_ramp = []
for name2, X_train2, X_val2, y_train2, y_val2 in datasets:
    x2 = feature_symbols(X_train2.shape[1])
    c2 = constant_symbols(7)
    time2 = x2[0]
    param2 = x2[1]
    log_time2 = sp.log(time2 + sp.sqrt(time2**2 + c2[2]))
    log_param2 = sp.log(param2 + c2[4])
    rate2 = c2[1] * param2 ** c2[6]
    half2 = c2[3] * log_param2 + c2[5]
    expr2 = c2[0] * sp.tanh(rate2 * (log_time2 - half2)) + c2[6]
    # Wait, c2[6] is the power AND the offset - conflict. Use 8 constants:
    c3 = constant_symbols(8)
    rate3 = c3[1] * param2 ** c3[7]
    half3 = c3[3] * log_param2 + c3[5]
    expr3 = c3[0] * sp.tanh(rate3 * (log_time2 - half3)) + c3[6]
    r3 = evaluate_expression(expr3, X_train2, y_train2, X_val2, y_val2,
        constants=c3, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0], max_nfev=500)
    results_no_ramp.append((r3['nmse_val'], name2))

results_no_ramp.sort(reverse=True)
for nmse, name2 in results_no_ramp[:5]:
    print(f'  {nmse:.4f}  {name2}')
print(f'Mean: {np.mean([r[0] for r in results_no_ramp]):.4f}')
" 2>&1 | tail -15

import numpy as np

# Parsimony: combined = fit_score * (1 - 0.2 * min(complexity/160, 1))
# Current: complexity=35, nmse=0.0376
# v3: complexity=37, nmse=0.0369

for label, complexity, nmse in [('current', 35, 0.0376), ('v3_10c', 37, 0.0369)]:
    fit_score = 1.0 / (1.0 + nmse)
    parsimony = 1.0 - 0.2 * min(complexity / 160.0, 1.0)
    combined = fit_score * parsimony
    print(f'{label}: fit={fit_score:.4f}, parsimony={parsimony:.4f}, combined={combined:.4f}')

# What about trying to reduce complexity while keeping good fit?
# Current template has 35 nodes. If we can get same nmse with 30 nodes, 
# parsimony improves.

# Let's also think about what's causing the Kar2011 Q37 to be hard.
# The Kar2011 Q37 has sequential x1 (3,4,6,8,9,22). 
# The key issue: the curves are very spread out in time (x0 spans 7700 to 937000)
# but the early growth is power-law-like (exponent ~2).
# The tanh-in-log-time model SHOULD capture this well.
# The problem might be that the optimizer is getting stuck.

# Let's test with more nfev on just Kar2011

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from pathlib import Path
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_dataset
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

base = '/home/mrsar/protein-ag/past-published-data/disease-relevant non-inhibited'
path = Path(base) / 'htt/Kar2011/Fits/Kar2011_Q37_sec/fit.tsv'
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = load_dataset(path)

x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
c = constant_symbols(9)
time = x[0]
parameter = x[1]
log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))
log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time)

# Try many different initial values
inits = [
    [0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0],   # current
    [0.5, 0.1, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0, 10.0, 0.5, 0.0, 2.0],   # high half-time
    [0.5, 0.01, 1.0, 2.0, 1.0, 12.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0],  # x0 in [7700,937000] -> log~12
    [0.5, 0.001, 1.0, 3.0, 1.0, 8.0, 0.0, 0.0, 3.0],  # strong power law
    [0.5, 1.0, 1.0, -1.0, 1.0, 15.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0],  # negative c3
    [1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0, 11.0, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0],
    [0.5, 0.1, 1.0, 1.5, 0.1, 9.0, 0.0, 0.0, 2.0],
]

best_nmse = 999
best_init = None
for init in inits:
    r = evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
        constants=c, initial_values=init, max_nfev=2000)
    print(f'init={init[:4]}...: nmse={r[\"nmse_val\"]:.4f}')
    if r['nmse_val'] < best_nmse:
        best_nmse = r['nmse_val']
        best_init = init
print(f'Best: {best_nmse:.4f} with {best_init}')
"

08:41:45 [agentic_generator] Step 19: tool=run_command returned:

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

datasets = load_all_datasets()

def eval_expr(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val, init):
    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
    c = constant_symbols(9)
    time = x[0]
    parameter = x[1]
    log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))
    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
    rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time)
    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
        constants=c, initial_values=init, max_nfev=1000)

# Test new initial values
new_init = [0.5, 0.1, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0, 10.0, 0.0, 0.0, 2.0]
old_init = [0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0]

results_new = []
results_old = []
for name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:
    r_new = eval_expr(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val, new_init)
    r_old = eval_expr(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val, old_init)
    results_new.append((r_new['nmse_val'], name))
    results_old.append((r_old['nmse_val'], name))

results_new.sort(reverse=True)
results_old.sort(reverse=True)
print('New init - Worst 10:')
for nmse, name in results_new[:10]:
    print(f'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}')
print(f'Mean nmse_val (new): {np.mean([r[0] for r in results_new]):.4f}')
print()
print(f'Mean nmse_val (old): {np.mean([r[0] for r in results_old]):.4f}')
" 2>&1 | tail -18

Worst 10:\')\nfor nmse, name in results[:10]:\n    print(f\'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}\')\nprint(f\'Mean nmse_val: {np.mean([r[0] for r in results]):.4f}\')\n" 2>&1 | tail -15' in /home/mrsar/protein-ag/skydiscover/benchmarks/pysr_symbolic (timeout=120s)
08:42:30 [agentic_generator] Step 20: tool=run_command returned:

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

datasets = load_all_datasets()

# Key insight: tanh(x0) is always saturated for large x0 datasets.
# Fix: use tanh(log_time - c7) instead - this uses the same log-time transform
# as the main sigmoid, so it adapts to any x0 scale automatically.
# Template: c0*tanh(c1*x1^c8*(log_time - c3*log(x1+c4) - c5)) + c6 + c7*tanh(log_time - c8)
# But c8 conflicts... use c9 instead -> 10 constants

# Alternative simpler fix: use tanh(c7*log_time) as secondary ramp
# This is scale-invariant because log_time is already compressed
# Template: c0*tanh(c1*x1^c8*(log_time - c3*log(x1+c4) - c5)) + c6 + c7*tanh(c8*log_time)
# 9 constants, same as current but secondary ramp uses log-time instead of raw time

def eval_logramp(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val):
    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
    c = constant_symbols(9)
    time = x[0]
    parameter = x[1]
    log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))
    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
    rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
    # Secondary ramp uses log_time - scale invariant!
    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(log_time)
    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
        constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)

results = []
for name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:
    r = eval_logramp(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
    results.append((r['nmse_val'], name))

results.sort(reverse=True)
print('Log-ramp version - Worst 10:')
for nmse, name in results[:10]:
    print(f'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}')
print(f'Mean nmse_val: {np.mean([r[0] for r in results]):.4f}')
" 2>&1 | tail -15

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets, load_dataset
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols
from pathlib import Path

base = '/home/mrsar/protein-ag/past-published-data/disease-relevant non-inhibited'

# The key insight: for Kar2011 Q37, x0 in [7700, 937000]
# log(7700) ~ 8.9, log(937000) ~ 13.7
# So the sigmoid half-point is around log(x0) ~ 11
# But c5=2.0 initial value puts half at log(x0)=2, way too early
# 
# Fix: make c5 represent the LOG of the half-time directly
# i.e., half_log_time = c3*log(x1+c4) + c5
# where c5 is initialized to log(median_x0) ~ 5 (typical for most datasets)
# But this is already the current structure! The problem is c5=2.0 initial.
#
# What if we initialize c5 to a larger value like 5.0?
# Let's test on all datasets

datasets = load_all_datasets()

def eval_expr(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val, c5_init):
    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
    c = constant_symbols(9)
    time = x[0]
    parameter = x[1]
    log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))
    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
    rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time)
    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
        constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, c5_init, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)

for c5_init in [2.0, 5.0, 7.0]:
    results = []
    for name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:
        r = eval_expr(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val, c5_init)
        results.append((r['nmse_val'], name))
    results.sort(reverse=True)
    mean_nmse = np.mean([r[0] for r in results])
    worst3 = [(nmse, name) for nmse, name in results[:3]]
    print(f'c5_init={c5_init}: mean={mean_nmse:.4f}, worst3={[(round(n,3), nm.split(\"/\")[-1]) for n,nm in worst3]}')
" 2>&1 | tail -10
[mode: exec]
[exit code: 0]

c5_init=5.0: mean=0.0663, worst3=[(1.058, 'Kakkar2016_6kDa_polyQ_prim'), (1.038, 'Kakkar2016_6kDa_polyQ_sec'), (0.589, 'Nielsen2001_seeded_sec')]
c5_init=7.0: mean=0.0433, worst3=[(1.009, 'ACE_sec'), (0.589, 'Nielsen2001_seeded_sec'), (0.241, 'Kar2011_Q37_prim')]

08:44:07 [agentic_generator] Step 22: tool=run_command args={'command': 'python3 -c "\nimport numpy as np\nimport sympy as sp\nimport sys\nsys.path.insert(0,...'}
08:44:07 [run_command_tool] run_command: executing 'python3 -c "\nimport numpy as np\nimport sympy as sp\nimport sys\nsys.path.insert(0, \'.\')\nfrom disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets\nfrom pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols\n\ndatasets = load_all_datasets()\n\n# Reparameterize: half_log_time = c3*log(x1+c4) + log(c5)\n# where c5>0 always. This means the half-time in log-space is log(c5).\n# Optimizer works multiplicatively on c5 rather than additively on c5.\n# For datasets with x0~hours (log~2), c5_init=exp(2)~7.4\n# For datasets with x0~seconds (log~11), c5 needs to reach exp(11)~60000\n# Multiplicative steps: 7.4 * k = 60000 -> k~8100 (large but feasible)\n# vs additive: 2 + k = 11 -> k=9 (small, should be easy!)\n# Actually additive is EASIER. So reparameterization won\'t help.\n#\n# The real issue: with c5=2.0 and Kar2011 needing c5~11, \n# the optimizer starts at a point where the sigmoid is fully saturated\n# (all predicted values ~1) because log(x0) >> c5=2 for ALL x0 in [7700, 937000].\n# This means the gradient is near zero everywhere -> stuck!\n#\n# Solution: normalize the sigmoid argument by the data range.\n# Use: sigmoid_arg = c1 * x1^c8 * (log_time/c5 - c3*log(x1+c4) - 1)\n# where c5 is now the SCALE of log_time (initialized to median log_time)\n# But this changes the template structure.\n#\n# Alternative: use the MEAN log-time as the reference\n# half_log_time = c3*log(x1+c4) + c5 * mean_log_time\n# But we can\'t use data statistics in the template.\n#\n# BEST APPROACH: Use a different secondary term that doesn\'t saturate\n# AND use a better initial value strategy.\n# \n# The secondary tanh(x0) term:\n# - For prp/Stoehr: x0 in [1e-14, 17] -> tanh(x0) works perfectly\n# - For alphasyn: x0 in [0, 29] -> tanh(x0) works  \n# - For Kar2011: x0 in [7700, 937000] -> tanh(x0) = 1 always -> c7 = constant\n#   This makes c6+c7 degenerate. The optimizer finds c6+c7=offset, but\n#   the gradient landscape is flat in the c7 direction -> slow convergence.\n#\n# Fix: use tanh(x0 * c7) where c7 is initialized to 1/median_x0 ~ 1e-5\n# But we need a constant for that, and c7 is already the coefficient.\n# Actually: c7*tanh(c8*x0) is 10 constants.\n# \n# Simplest fix that keeps 9 constants: drop x1 power law from rate,\n# use c8 for the ramp timescale instead.\n# But we showed that hurts (mean 0.052).\n#\n# What if we use tanh(x0/(x0+c7)) instead? This is bounded [0,1) for x0>0\n# and equals x0/(x0+c7) for small x0. But x0/(x0+c7) = 1-c7/(x0+c7)\n# which saturates to 1 for large x0 just like tanh.\n# \n# Actually the REAL solution: for datasets where tanh(x0)=1 always,\n# the c7 term just adds a constant, which is already captured by c6.\n# So the optimizer should set c7=0 and use c6 for the offset.\n# But it doesn\'t because c0*tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c6 + c7 is degenerate\n# when tanh(x0)=1.\n#\n# Let me just verify: does removing c7*tanh(x0) and using 8 constants \n# (no secondary ramp, just c0*tanh + c6) hurt the key datasets?\n\ndef eval_noramp(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val):\n    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])\n    c = constant_symbols(8)\n    time = x[0]\n    parameter = x[1]\n    log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))\n    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])\n    rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[7]\n    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]\n    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)\n    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6]\n    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,\n        constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)\n\nresults = []\nfor name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:\n    r = eval_noramp(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)\n    results.append((r[\'nmse_val\'], name))\nresults.sort(reverse=True)\nprint(\'No-ramp (8 constants) - worst 10:\')\nfor nmse, name in results[:10]:\n    print(f\'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}\')\nprint(f\'Mean: {np.mean([r[0] for r in results]):.4f}\')\n# Also check complexity\nprint(f\'Complexity: {results[0]}\')\n" 2>&1 | tail -15' in /home/mrsar/protein-ag/skydiscover/benchmarks/pysr_symbolic (timeout=120s)

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

datasets = load_all_datasets()

# Reparameterize: half_log_time = c3*log(x1+c4) + log(c5)
# where c5>0 always. This means the half-time in log-space is log(c5).
# Optimizer works multiplicatively on c5 rather than additively on c5.
# For datasets with x0~hours (log~2), c5_init=exp(2)~7.4
# For datasets with x0~seconds (log~11), c5 needs to reach exp(11)~60000
# Multiplicative steps: 7.4 * k = 60000 -> k~8100 (large but feasible)
# vs additive: 2 + k = 11 -> k=9 (small, should be easy!)
# Actually additive is EASIER. So reparameterization won't help.
#
# The real issue: with c5=2.0 and Kar2011 needing c5~11, 
# the optimizer starts at a point where the sigmoid is fully saturated
# (all predicted values ~1) because log(x0) >> c5=2 for ALL x0 in [7700, 937000].
# This means the gradient is near zero everywhere -> stuck!
#
# Solution: normalize the sigmoid argument by the data range.
# Use: sigmoid_arg = c1 * x1^c8 * (log_time/c5 - c3*log(x1+c4) - 1)
# where c5 is now the SCALE of log_time (initialized to median log_time)
# But this changes the template structure.
#
# Alternative: use the MEAN log-time as the reference
# half_log_time = c3*log(x1+c4) + c5 * mean_log_time
# But we can't use data statistics in the template.
#
# BEST APPROACH: Use a different secondary term that doesn't saturate
# AND use a better initial value strategy.
# 
# The secondary tanh(x0) term:
# - For prp/Stoehr: x0 in [1e-14, 17] -> tanh(x0) works perfectly
# - For alphasyn: x0 in [0, 29] -> tanh(x0) works  
# - For Kar2011: x0 in [7700, 937000] -> tanh(x0) = 1 always -> c7 = constant
#   This makes c6+c7 degenerate. The optimizer finds c6+c7=offset, but
#   the gradient landscape is flat in the c7 direction -> slow convergence.
#
# Fix: use tanh(x0 * c7) where c7 is initialized to 1/median_x0 ~ 1e-5
# But we need a constant for that, and c7 is already the coefficient.
# Actually: c7*tanh(c8*x0) is 10 constants.
# 
# Simplest fix that keeps 9 constants: drop x1 power law from rate,
# use c8 for the ramp timescale instead.
# But we showed that hurts (mean 0.052).
#
# What if we use tanh(x0/(x0+c7)) instead? This is bounded [0,1) for x0>0
# and equals x0/(x0+c7) for small x0. But x0/(x0+c7) = 1-c7/(x0+c7)
# which saturates to 1 for large x0 just like tanh.
# 
# Actually the REAL solution: for datasets where tanh(x0)=1 always,
# the c7 term just adds a constant, which is already captured by c6.
# So the optimizer should set c7=0 and use c6 for the offset.
# But it doesn't because c0*tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c6 + c7 is degenerate
# when tanh(x0)=1.
#
# Let me just verify: does removing c7*tanh(x0) and using 8 constants 
# (no secondary ramp, just c0*tanh + c6) hurt the key datasets?

def eval_noramp(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val):
    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
    c = constant_symbols(8)
    time = x[0]
    parameter = x[1]
    log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))
    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
    rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[7]
    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6]
    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
        constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)

results = []
for name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:
    r = eval_noramp(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
    results.append((r['nmse_val'], name))
results.sort(reverse=True)
print('No-ramp (8 constants) - worst 10:')
for nmse, name in results[:10]:
    print(f'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}')
print(f'Mean: {np.mean([r[0] for r in results]):.4f}')
# Also check complexity
print(f'Complexity: {results[0]}')

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from pathlib import Path
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_dataset
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

base = '/home/mrsar/protein-ag/past-published-data/disease-relevant non-inhibited'
path = Path(base) / 'htt/Kar2011/Fits/Kar2011_Q37_sec/fit.tsv'
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = load_dataset(path)

x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
c = constant_symbols(9)
time = x[0]
parameter = x[1]
log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))
log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time)
r = evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
    constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)
print('Fitted constants:', r['constants'])
# c0=95, c1=0.0025, c2=1, c3=1.5, c4=-1.6, c5=-3.9, c6=-1.6, c7=-2.1, c8=0.13
# c0=95 is huge! c6=-1.6, c7=-2.1 are large negative offsets
# The expression: 95*tanh(0.0025*x1^0.13*(log(x0+sqrt(x0^2+1)) - 1.5*log(x1-1.6) + 3.9)) - 1.6 - 2.1
# = 95*tanh(...) - 3.7
# For this to give y in [0,1]: 95*tanh(z) - 3.7 must be in [0,1]
# tanh(z) in [3.7/95, 4.7/95] = [0.039, 0.049] -> z very small
# This means the sigmoid is nearly linear, not S-shaped!
# The model is using the LINEAR part of tanh to fit a power-law growth.

# Let's understand: what shape do the Kar2011 curves actually have?
# For x1=22 (fastest), y goes from ~0.003 at x0=17000 to ~1.0 at x0=900000
# That's a 300x range in x0 for a 300x range in y -> nearly power law!
# log(x0) goes from 9.7 to 13.7 -> 4 units
# A sigmoid in log-time would need rate*4 ~ pi/2 (to go 0->1 in 4 units)
# rate = c1*x1^c8 = 0.0025*22^0.13 ~ 0.003 -> sigmoid_arg range = 0.003*4 = 0.012
# That's WAY too small for a sigmoid! The model is nearly linear.

# The real issue: the Kar2011 Q37 curves don't look sigmoidal at all in log-time space.
# They look like power laws (straight lines in log-log space).
# Our model can approximate this with a nearly-linear tanh, but poorly.

# What if we use a POWER LAW model directly?
# y = c0 * (x0/c5)^c1 / (1 + (x0/c5)^c1) -- Hill function
# = c0 / (1 + (c5/x0)^c1)
# This is a sigmoid in log-time space with FIXED rate=c1

# Actually this is exactly what our model does when sigmoid_arg = c1*(log(x0) - log(c5))
# The problem is the rate c1*x1^c8 is too small.

# Let's check: what rate would give a good fit?
# For x1=22, the curve goes from y=0.003 at x0=17000 to y=1 at x0=900000
# In log-time: from 9.7 to 13.7 (range=4)
# For tanh to go from -1 to 1 in this range: rate*4 ~ 3 -> rate~0.75
# But fitted rate = c1*x1^c8 = 0.0025*22^0.13 ~ 0.003 !!
# The rate is 250x too small.

# Why? Because c4=-1.6 makes log(x1+c4) = log(x1-1.6) which is large for x1=22
# half_log_time = 1.5*log(22-1.6) + (-3.9) = 1.5*3.01 - 3.9 = 0.62
# But actual half-log-time for x1=22 should be ~11.7 (midpoint of 9.7,13.7)
# So the half-time is completely wrong!
# The fitted c5=-3.9 makes half_log_time = 1.5*log(x1-1.6) - 3.9
# For x1=22: 1.5*3.01 - 3.9 = 0.62 (way off from needed 11.7)
# For this to equal 11.7: c5 needs to be 11.7 - 1.5*3.01 = 7.2

# So the optimizer needs c5~7.2 but starts at c5=2.0 and gets stuck.
# The gradient: d(nmse)/d(c5) at c5=2.0 is near zero because
# sigmoid is already saturated (all tanh ~ +-1) at c5=2.0.

# CONCLUSION: The issue is gradient vanishing when sigmoid is saturated.
# The fix: use a BETTER INITIAL VALUE for c5 that puts the sigmoid in the linear region.
# For Kar2011: c5~7-12. But for most datasets: c5~2-5.
# We need a single initial value that works for all.

# What if c5_init = 5.0 (middle ground)?
print('Testing c5_init=5.0 on Kar2011:')
r2 = evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
    constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=2000)
print('nmse_val:', r2['nmse_val'])
print('constants:', r2['constants'])

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

datasets = load_all_datasets()

# New idea: use log1p(x0/c5) as time transform
# This normalizes x0 by c5, so c5_init=1 and optimizer adjusts c5 to match x0 scale
# For Kar2011 (x0~100000): log1p(100000/c5) - optimizer sets c5~10000
# For small datasets (x0~10): log1p(10/c5) - optimizer sets c5~1
# The sigmoid half-time is then: c3*log(x1+c4) + c5_half (different constant)
# Template: c0*tanh(c1*x1^c8*(log1p(x0/c2) - c3*log(x1+c4) - c5)) + c6 + c7*tanh(x0)
# 9 constants, c2 is the time scale (initialized to 1)

def eval_log1p(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val):
    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
    c = constant_symbols(9)
    time = x[0]
    parameter = x[1]
    # log1p(x0/c2) = log(1 + x0/c2) - scale-normalized log time
    log_time = sp.log(time / c[2] + 1)  # equivalent to log1p(x0/c2)
    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
    rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time)
    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
        constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)

results = []
for name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:
    r = eval_log1p(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
    results.append((r['nmse_val'], name))

results.sort(reverse=True)
print('log1p(x0/c2) version - worst 10:')
for nmse, name in results[:10]:
    print(f'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}')
print(f'Mean: {np.mean([r[0] for r in results]):.4f}')
" 2>&1 | tail -15

08:46:44 [run_command_tool] run_command: executing 'python3 -c "\nimport numpy as np\nimport sympy as sp\nimport sys\nsys.path.insert(0, \'.\')\nfrom disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets\nfrom pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols\n\ndatasets = load_all_datasets()\n\n# Key structural insight:\n# Current: c1*x1^c8*(log(x0+sqrt(x0^2+c2)) - c3*log(x1+c4) - c5)\n# The half-time offset c5 is in LOG-time space. For Kar2011, log(half_time)~11, \n# but c5_init=2 -> sigmoid is saturated at start -> gradient vanishes.\n#\n# New: use log(x0+sqrt(x0^2+c2)) - log(c5+sqrt(c5^2+c2)) as the time variable\n# = log((x0+sqrt(x0^2+c2))/(c5+sqrt(c5^2+c2)))\n# This is the DIFFERENCE of two asinh transforms.\n# When x0=c5: the argument is 0 -> sigmoid is at midpoint -> NEVER saturated at init!\n# c5 now represents the ACTUAL half-time (same units as x0), not log-half-time.\n# c5_init=1 means half-time at x0=1 (any scale).\n# The optimizer can adjust c5 to any scale multiplicatively.\n#\n# But this adds complexity (extra sqrt and log).\n# Alternative: just use c5 as a SCALE factor:\n# sigmoid_arg = c1*x1^c8*(log(x0+sqrt(x0^2+1)) - log(c5*x1^c3 + sqrt((c5*x1^c3)^2+1)))\n# Too complex.\n#\n# Simplest fix: replace c5 (offset) with log(c5) where c5>0\n# i.e., half_log_time = c3*log(x1+c4) + log(c5)\n# Now c5 is the HALF-TIME in asinh space (positive), c5_init=exp(2)~7.4\n# For Kar2011: half-time in asinh space ~ exp(11) ~ 60000 (the actual half-time in seconds)\n# The optimizer multiplies c5 by 60000/7.4 ~ 8000 - multiplicative, not additive\n# This is actually HARDER for the optimizer (large multiplicative step)\n# vs additive: c5 needs to go from 2 to 11 (step of 9)\n# Multiplicative: c5 needs to go from 7.4 to 60000 (factor of 8000)\n# So this doesn\'t help.\n#\n# REAL FIX: Use the half-time parameterization directly in the sigmoid:\n# sigmoid_arg = c1*x1^c8 * log((x0 + sqrt(x0^2+c2)) / (c5*x1^c3 + sqrt((c5*x1^c3)^2+c2)))\n# = c1*x1^c8 * (asinh_time - asinh_halftime)\n# where c5 is the ACTUAL half-time. c5_init=1.\n# For Kar2011: optimizer sets c5~100000 (the actual half-time in seconds)\n# But this requires c5*x1^c3 which adds complexity.\n#\n# SIMPLEST APPROACH: Keep the same structure but use c5 = log(half_time)\n# i.e., half_log_time = c3*log(x1+c4) + c5 where c5 = log(actual_half_time)\n# This is ALREADY the current structure. The issue is just the initial value.\n#\n# Let me try a COMPLETELY DIFFERENT approach for the half-time:\n# Use c5 as a MULTIPLIER of the log-time:\n# sigmoid_arg = c1*x1^c8 * (log_time - c5*c3*log(x1+c4) - c5)\n# = c1*x1^c8 * c5 * (log_time/c5 - c3*log(x1+c4) - 1)\n# Now c5 scales the ENTIRE log-time axis.\n# With c5=1: same as having half_log_time = c3*log(x1+c4) + 1 (fixed at 1)\n# The optimizer adjusts c5 to scale log-time.\n# For Kar2011: c5~11 (scales log_time so half is at 1)\n# For small datasets: c5~2-3\n\n# Actually this is just: rate\' = c1*x1^c8*c5, half\' = c5*(c3*log(x1+c4)+1)\n# = c1\'*x1^c8 * (log_time - c3\'*log(x1+c4) - c5\')\n# where c1\' = c1*c5, c3\' = c3, c5\' = c5\n# So it\'s equivalent to the current parameterization with different initial values.\n# The optimizer should find the same solution.\n\n# NEW STRUCTURAL IDEA: Use the RATIO x0/c5 inside the log:\n# log_time = log(x0/c5 + sqrt((x0/c5)^2 + 1))\n# = asinh(x0/c5)\n# This normalizes x0 by c5. With c5_init=1, for Kar2011 the optimizer sets c5~median(x0)~100000\n# The sigmoid half-point is then: c3*log(x1+c4) + c5_half\n# where c5_half is in NORMALIZED log-time space (always ~0-3 range)\n# This should work!\n\ndef eval_normalized(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val):\n    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])\n    c = constant_symbols(9)\n    time = x[0]\n    parameter = x[1]\n    # Normalized asinh: c2 is the time scale\n    t_norm = time / c[2]\n    log_time = sp.log(t_norm + sp.sqrt(t_norm**2 + 1))  # asinh(x0/c2)\n    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])\n    rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]\n    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]\n    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)\n    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time / c[2])\n    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,\n        constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)\n\nresults = []\nfor name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:\n    r = eval_normalized(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)\n    results.append((r[\'nmse_val\'], name))\n\nresults.sort(reverse=True)\nprint(\'Normalized asinh version - worst 10:\')\nfor nmse, name in results[:10]:\n    print(f\'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}\')\nprint(f\'Mean: {np.mean([r[0] for r in results]):.4f}\')\n" 2>&1 | tail -15' in /home/mrsar/protein-ag/skydiscover/benchmarks/pysr_symbolic (timeout=120s)
08:47:01 [agentic_generator] Step 25: tool=run_command returned:
$ python3 -c "
import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

datasets = load_all_datasets()

# Key structural insight:
# Current: c1*x1^c8*(log(x0+sqrt(x0^2+c2)) - c3*log(x1+c4) - c5)
# The half-time offset c5 is in LOG-time space. For Kar2011, log(half_time)~11, 
# but c5_init=2 -> sigmoid is saturated at start -> gradient vanishes.
#
# New: use log(x0+sqrt(x0^2+c2)) - log(c5+sqrt(c5^2+c2)) as the time variable
# = log((x0+sqrt(x0^2+c2))/(c5+sqrt(c5^2+c2)))
# This is the DIFFERENCE of two asinh transforms.
# When x0=c5: the argument is 0 -> sigmoid is at midpoint -> NEVER saturated at init!
# c5 now represents the ACTUAL half-time (same units as x0), not log-half-time.
# c5_init=1 means half-time at x0=1 (any scale).
# The optimizer can adjust c5 to any scale multiplicatively.
#
# But this adds complexity (extra sqrt and log).
# Alternative: just use c5 as a SCALE factor:
# sigmoid_arg = c1*x1^c8*(log(x0+sqrt(x0^2+1)) - log(c5*x1^c3 + sqrt((c5*x1^c3)^2+1)))
# Too complex.
#
# Simplest fix: replace c5 (offset) with log(c5) where c5>0
# i.e., half_log_time = c3*log(x1+c4) + log(c5)
# Now c5 is the HALF-TIME in asinh space (positive), c5_init=exp(2)~7.4
# For Kar2011: half-time in asinh space ~ exp(11) ~ 60000 (the actual half-time in seconds)
# The optimizer multiplies c5 by 60000/7.4 ~ 8000 - multiplicative, not additive
# This is actually HARDER for the optimizer (large multiplicative step)
# vs additive: c5 needs to go from 2 to 11 (step of 9)
# Multiplicative: c5 needs to go from 7.4 to 60000 (factor of 8000)
# So this doesn't help.
#
# REAL FIX: Use the half-time parameterization directly in the sigmoid:
# sigmoid_arg = c1*x1^c8 * log((x0 + sqrt(x0^2+c2)) / (c5*x1^c3 + sqrt((c5*x1^c3)^2+c2)))
# = c1*x1^c8 * (asinh_time - asinh_halftime)
# where c5 is the ACTUAL half-time. c5_init=1.
# For Kar2011: optimizer sets c5~100000 (the actual half-time in seconds)
# But this requires c5*x1^c3 which adds complexity.
#
# SIMPLEST APPROACH: Keep the same structure but use c5 = log(half_time)
# i.e., half_log_time = c3*log(x1+c4) + c5 where c5 = log(actual_half_time)
# This is ALREADY the current structure. The issue is just the initial value.
#
# Let me try a COMPLETELY DIFFERENT approach for the half-time:
# Use c5 as a MULTIPLIER of the log-time:
# sigmoid_arg = c1*x1^c8 * (log_time - c5*c3*log(x1+c4) - c5)
# = c1*x1^c8 * c5 * (log_time/c5 - c3*log(x1+c4) - 1)
# Now c5 scales the ENTIRE log-time axis.
# With c5=1: same as having half_log_time = c3*log(x1+c4) + 1 (fixed at 1)
# The optimizer adjusts c5 to scale log-time.
# For Kar2011: c5~11 (scales log_time so half is at 1)
# For small datasets: c5~2-3

# Actually this is just: rate' = c1*x1^c8*c5, half' = c5*(c3*log(x1+c4)+1)
# = c1'*x1^c8 * (log_time - c3'*log(x1+c4) - c5')
# where c1' = c1*c5, c3' = c3, c5' = c5
# So it's equivalent to the current parameterization with different initial values.
# The optimizer should find the same solution.

# NEW STRUCTURAL IDEA: Use the RATIO x0/c5 inside the log:
# log_time = log(x0/c5 + sqrt((x0/c5)^2 + 1))
# = asinh(x0/c5)
# This normalizes x0 by c5. With c5_init=1, for Kar2011 the optimizer sets c5~median(x0)~100000
# The sigmoid half-point is then: c3*log(x1+c4) + c5_half
# where c5_half is in NORMALIZED log-time space (always ~0-3 range)
# This should work!

def eval_normalized(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val):
    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
    c = constant_symbols(9)
    time = x[0]
    parameter = x[1]
    # Normalized asinh: c2 is the time scale
    t_norm = time / c[2]
    log_time = sp.log(t_norm + sp.sqrt(t_norm**2 + 1))  # asinh(x0/c2)
    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
    rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time / c[2])
    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
        constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)

results = []
for name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:
    r = eval_normalized(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
    results.append((r['nmse_val'], name))

results.sort(reverse=True)
print('Normalized asinh version - worst 10:')
for nmse, name in results[:10]:
    print(f'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}')
print(f'Mean: {np.mean([r[0] for r in results]):.4f}')
" 2>&1 | tail -15

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

datasets = load_all_datasets()

# Get complexity for normalized version
name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = datasets[0]
x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
c = constant_symbols(9)
time = x[0]
parameter = x[1]
t_norm = time / c[2]
log_time = sp.log(t_norm + sp.sqrt(t_norm**2 + 1))
log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time / c[2])
r = evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
    constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)
print('Complexity:', r['complexity'])

# Compute expected combined score
nmse = 0.0346
complexity = r['complexity']
fit_score = 1.0 / (1.0 + nmse)
parsimony = 1.0 - 0.2 * min(complexity / 160.0, 1.0)
combined = fit_score * parsimony
print(f'Expected: fit={fit_score:.4f}, parsimony={parsimony:.4f}, combined={combined:.4f}')
print(f'Current:  fit=0.9637, parsimony=0.9563, combined=0.9216')

# Also check: does the normalized version handle negative x0 (Ye2011)?
# asinh(x0/c2) is defined for all real x0, so yes.
# For x0=-257, c2=1: asinh(-257) = log(-257 + sqrt(257^2+1)) = log(-257+257.002) ~ log(0.002) ~ -6.2
# That's fine.
print()
print('Checking negative x0 handling:')

c2_val = 1.0
t_norm_val = x0_neg / c2_val
log_t = np.log(t_norm_val + np.sqrt(t_norm_val**2 + 1))
print(f'asinh(-257/1) = {log_t:.4f} (should be finite)')

half_log_time)\nexpression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time)\nr0 = evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,\n    constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=100)\nprint(\'v4 complexity:\', r0[\'complexity\'])\n\nresults = []\nfor name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:\n    r = eval_v4(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)\n    results.append((r[\'nmse_val\'], name))\nresults.sort(reverse=True)\nmean_nmse = np.mean([r[0] for r in results])\ncomplexity_v4 = 41  # estimated\nfit_score = 1.0 / (1.0 + mean_nmse)\nparsimony = 1.0 - 0.2 * min(complexity_v4 / 160.0, 1.0)\ncombined = fit_score * parsimony\nprint(\'v4 (normalized log, raw tanh ramp) - worst 10:\')\nfor nmse, name in results[:10]:\n    print(f\'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}\')\nprint(f\'Mean nmse: {mean_nmse:.4f}, est combined: {combined:.4f}\')\n" 2>&1 | tail -18' in /home/mrsar/protein-ag/skydiscover/benchmarks/pysr_symbolic (timeout=120s)
08:48:00 [agentic_generator] Step 27: tool=run_command returned:

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

datasets = load_all_datasets()

# The normalized version is better in nmse but worse in complexity.
# Key insight: the secondary ramp tanh(time/c2) reuses c2 from the log transform.
# This is what makes it 47 nodes vs 35.
# 
# What if we keep tanh(x0) (not tanh(x0/c2)) but use the normalized log for main sigmoid?
# Template: c0*tanh(c1*x1^c8*(log(x0/c2+sqrt((x0/c2)^2+1)) - c3*log(x1+c4) - c5)) + c6 + c7*tanh(x0)
# The secondary ramp tanh(x0) is still scale-dependent, but the main sigmoid is normalized.
# Complexity should be less than 47.

def eval_v4(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val):
    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
    c = constant_symbols(9)
    time = x[0]
    parameter = x[1]
    t_norm = time / c[2]
    log_time = sp.log(t_norm + sp.sqrt(t_norm**2 + 1))
    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
    rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time)
    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
        constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)

# Get complexity
name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = datasets[0]
x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
c = constant_symbols(9)
time = x[0]
parameter = x[1]
t_norm = time / c[2]
log_time = sp.log(t_norm + sp.sqrt(t_norm**2 + 1))
log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time)
r0 = evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
    constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=100)
print('v4 complexity:', r0['complexity'])

results = []
for name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:
    r = eval_v4(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
    results.append((r['nmse_val'], name))
results.sort(reverse=True)
mean_nmse = np.mean([r[0] for r in results])
complexity_v4 = 41  # estimated
fit_score = 1.0 / (1.0 + mean_nmse)
parsimony = 1.0 - 0.2 * min(complexity_v4 / 160.0, 1.0)
combined = fit_score * parsimony
print('v4 (normalized log, raw tanh ramp) - worst 10:')
for nmse, name in results[:10]:
    print(f'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}')
print(f'Mean nmse: {mean_nmse:.4f}, est combined: {combined:.4f}')
" 2>&1 | tail -18

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_all_datasets
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

datasets = load_all_datasets()

# Use log(x0 + sqrt(x0^2 + c2^2)) where c2 is a scale parameter
# = log(c2 * (x0/c2 + sqrt((x0/c2)^2 + 1)))
# = log(c2) + asinh(x0/c2)
# So the half-time offset becomes: c5_new = c5 - log(c2)
# The optimizer adjusts c2 to normalize x0, and c5 absorbs log(c2).
# With c2_init=1: same as current with c2=1 initially.
# The key: c2 is multiplicatively coupled to x0, so optimizer can scale it freely.
# Complexity: same as current (35) because c2**2 replaces c[2] (same node count).

def eval_c2sq(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val):
    x = feature_symbols(X_train.shape[1])
    c = constant_symbols(9)
    time = x[0]
    parameter = x[1]
    log_time = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]**2))
    log_param = sp.log(parameter + c[4])
    rate = c[1] * parameter ** c[8]
    half_log_time = c[3] * log_param + c[5]
    sigmoid_arg = rate * (log_time - half_log_time)
    expression = c[0] * sp.tanh(sigmoid_arg) + c[6] + c[7] * sp.tanh(time)
    return evaluate_expression(expression, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val,
        constants=c, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=1000)

# Check complexity
name0, X_train0, X_val0, y_train0, y_val0 = datasets[0]
x0 = feature_symbols(X_train0.shape[1])
c0 = constant_symbols(9)
t0 = x0[0]; p0 = x0[1]
lt0 = sp.log(t0 + sp.sqrt(t0**2 + c0[2]**2))
lp0 = sp.log(p0 + c0[4])
rt0 = c0[1] * p0 ** c0[8]
ht0 = c0[3] * lp0 + c0[5]
sa0 = rt0 * (lt0 - ht0)
ex0 = c0[0] * sp.tanh(sa0) + c0[6] + c0[7] * sp.tanh(t0)
r0 = evaluate_expression(ex0, X_train0, y_train0, X_val0, y_val0,
    constants=c0, initial_values=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0], max_nfev=100)
print('c2^2 version complexity:', r0['complexity'])

results = []
for name, X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val in datasets:
    r = eval_c2sq(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
    results.append((r['nmse_val'], name))
results.sort(reverse=True)
mean_nmse = np.mean([r[0] for r in results])
complexity = r0['complexity']
fit_score = 1.0 / (1.0 + mean_nmse)
parsimony = 1.0 - 0.2 * min(complexity / 160.0, 1.0)
combined = fit_score * parsimony
print('c2^2 version - worst 10:')
for nmse, name in results[:10]:
    print(f'  {nmse:.4f}  {name}')
print(f'Mean nmse: {mean_nmse:.4f}, complexity: {complexity}, combined: {combined:.4f}')
" 2>&1 | tail -15

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from pathlib import Path
from disease_relevant_noninhibited_all.evaluator import load_dataset, _load_fit_table
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, evaluate_expression, feature_symbols

base = '/home/mrsar/protein-ag/past-published-data/disease-relevant non-inhibited'

# Look at Kakkar2016 6kDa with seed - what's the shape?
path = Path(base) / 'htt/Kakkar2016/FIts/Kakkar2016_6kDa_polyQ_with_seed_sec/fit.tsv'
X, y = _load_fit_table(path)
print('Kakkar2016 6kDa with seed:')
print('  x1 unique:', np.unique(X[:,1]))
print('  x0 range:', X[:,0].min(), X[:,0].max())

# Look at a specific curve
for x1_val in sorted(np.unique(X[:,1]))[:2]:
    mask = X[:,1] == x1_val
    x0s = X[mask,0]
    ys = y[mask]
    idx = np.argsort(x0s)
    print(f'  x1={x1_val}: first 5:', list(zip(x0s[idx[:5]].round(0), ys[idx[:5]].round(4))))
    print(f'  last 3:', list(zip(x0s[idx[-3:]].round(0), ys[idx[-3:]].round(4))))

# What's the log-log slope?
print()
x1_val = sorted(np.unique(X[:,1]))[-1]  # highest x1
mask = X[:,1] == x1_val
x0s = X[mask,0]
ys = y[mask]
idx = np.argsort(x0s)
x0s_s = x0s[idx]
ys_s = ys[idx]
print(f'x1={x1_val} log-log:')
for x0, yv in zip(x0s_s[:8], ys_s[:8]):
    if yv > 0 and x0 > 0:
        print(f'  log10(x0)={np.log10(x0):.2f}, log10(y)={np.log10(yv):.2f}')

08:49:47 [agentic_generator] Step 30: tool=run_command returned:

import sympy as sp
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from pysr_harness.equation_session import constant_symbols, feature_symbols

def count_nodes(expr):
    return sum(1 for _ in sp.preorder_traversal(expr))

x = feature_symbols(2)
c = constant_symbols(12)
time = x[0]
parameter = x[1]

# Current (complexity=35):
lt_curr = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]))
expr_curr = c[0]*sp.tanh(c[1]*parameter**c[8]*(lt_curr - c[3]*sp.log(parameter+c[4]) - c[5])) + c[6] + c[7]*sp.tanh(time)
print(f'Current: {count_nodes(expr_curr)} nodes')

# v4 normalized (complexity=43):
t_norm = time / c[2]
lt_norm = sp.log(t_norm + sp.sqrt(t_norm**2 + 1))
expr_v4 = c[0]*sp.tanh(c[1]*parameter**c[8]*(lt_norm - c[3]*sp.log(parameter+c[4]) - c[5])) + c[6] + c[7]*sp.tanh(time)
print(f'v4 (normalized log, raw tanh): {count_nodes(expr_v4)} nodes')

# v4b: normalized log + normalized ramp (complexity=47):
expr_v4b = c[0]*sp.tanh(c[1]*parameter**c[8]*(lt_norm - c[3]*sp.log(parameter+c[4]) - c[5])) + c[6] + c[7]*sp.tanh(time/c[2])
print(f'v4b (normalized log + normalized ramp): {count_nodes(expr_v4b)} nodes')

# Can we reduce complexity of v4? 
# Note: log(t/c2 + sqrt((t/c2)^2 + 1)) = log((t + sqrt(t^2 + c2^2))/c2)
# = log(t + sqrt(t^2 + c2^2)) - log(c2)
# So v4 = log(t + sqrt(t^2 + c2^2)) - log(c2) which is equivalent to current with c5 -> c5+log(c2)
# The complexity of log(t + sqrt(t^2 + c2^2)) is same as current!
# And log(c2) gets absorbed into c5.
# So v4 is EQUIVALENT to current with c2^2 in the sqrt!

lt_equiv = sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]**2))  # = log(c2) + asinh(t/c2)
expr_equiv = c[0]*sp.tanh(c[1]*parameter**c[8]*(lt_equiv - c[3]*sp.log(parameter+c[4]) - c[5])) + c[6] + c[7]*sp.tanh(time)
print(f'Equivalent c2^2 form: {count_nodes(expr_equiv)} nodes')

# So v4 with raw tanh(time) is equivalent to c2^2 form!
# The c2^2 form has complexity 37 and nmse=0.0358.
# The v4 form has complexity 43 and nmse=0.0352.
# Why different? Because v4 uses time/c[2] which creates more nodes.

# What about: keep c2^2 form but initialize c2 differently?
# c2^2 form: log(time + sqrt(time^2 + c2^2)) = log(c2) + asinh(time/c2)
# With c2_init=1: asinh(time/1) = asinh(time)
# For Kar2011 (time~100000): asinh(100000) ~ log(200000) ~ 12.2
# This is the SAME as current with c2_init=1!
# The difference: c5 absorbs log(c2), so c5_init=2 with c2_init=1 
# means half-time at asinh(time)=2, i.e., time~sinh(2)~3.6
# For Kar2011: half-time at time~3.6 (way too early for x0~100000)
# The optimizer needs to either: increase c5 OR decrease c2.
# If c2 decreases to 0.001: log(0.001) + asinh(100000/0.001) ~ -6.9 + 18.7 = 11.8 ✓
# So c2^2 form CAN work if optimizer decreases c2!
# But with c2_init=1, it starts at a bad point.
# 
# With c2_init=1000 (for large x0): 
# log(1000) + asinh(100000/1000) = 6.9 + asinh(100) ~ 6.9 + 5.3 = 12.2 ✓
# But for small datasets (x0~10): log(1000) + asinh(10/1000) ~ 6.9 + 0.01 = 6.9 (too large)
# 
# Conclusion: c2^2 form is equivalent to current but with different c2 parameterization.
# Neither is better structurally.

print()
print('The v4 with time/c2 IS different from c2^2 form because:')
print('v4 uses: log(time/c2 + sqrt((time/c2)^2 + 1))')
print('c2^2 uses: log(time + sqrt(time^2 + c2^2)) = log(c2) + log(time/c2 + sqrt((time/c2)^2+1))')
print('Difference: just log(c2) which gets absorbed into c5.')
print('So they ARE equivalent! The complexity difference is from how sympy counts nodes.')
print()
print('v4 node count detail:')
print('  t/c2:', count_nodes(time/c[2]))
print('  (t/c2)^2:', count_nodes((time/c[2])**2))
print('  sqrt((t/c2)^2+1):', count_nodes(sp.sqrt((time/c[2])**2 + 1)))
print('  log(t/c2 + sqrt((t/c2)^2+1)):', count_nodes(sp.log(time/c[2] + sp.sqrt((time/c[2])**2 + 1))))
print('  log(t + sqrt(t^2+c2^2)):', count_nodes(sp.log(time + sp.sqrt(time**2 + c[2]**2))))
"
